sanskrit_devanagari:
कर्मजं बुद्धियुक्ता हि फलं त्यक्त्वा मनीषिणः ।
जन्मबन्धविनिर्मुक्ताः पदं गच्छन्त्यनामयम् ॥ ५१ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
karma-jaṁ buddhi-yuktā hi phalaṁ tyaktvā manīṣiṇaḥ |
janma-bandha-vinirmuktāḥ padaṁ gacchanty anāmayam || 51 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence byte-identical to canonical)
exact (text body identical; single danda terminator)
exact (text body identical; verse-number marker rendered as ।।2.51।।)
IAST exact (karma-jaṁ buddhi-yuktā hi phalaṁ tyaktvā manīṣiṇaḥ / janma-bandha-vinirmuktāḥ padaṁ gacchanty anāmayam)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_02.051 = MBh_06,024.051). Body text identical: 'karmajaṃ buddhiyuktā hi phalaṃ tyaktvā manīṣiṇaḥ / janmabandhavinirmuktāḥ padaṃ gacchanty anāmayam'. GRETIL writes the compounds without hyphens; semantic content identical.

anvaya.meaning: born of (in compound karma-jam: 'born of action')
joined / united (plural; in compound buddhi-yuktāḥ: 'those joined to buddhi')
the wise / those endowed with intellect
completely freed (in compound janma-bandha-vinirmuktāḥ: 'freed from the bondage of birth')

translations.verbatim_quote: By thus engaging in devotional service to the Lord, great sages or devotees free themselves from the results of work in the material world. In this way they become free from the cycle of birth and death and attain the state beyond all miseries [by going back to Godhead].

The wise endowed with equanimity of intellect, abandon attachment to the fruits of actions, which bind one to the cycle of life and death. By working in such consciousness, they attain the state beyond all suffering.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The liberated living entities belong to that place where there are no material miseries. The Bhāgavatam (10.14.58) says: 'For one who has accepted the boat of the lotus feet of the Lord, who is the shelter of the cosmic manifestation and is famous as Mukunda, the ocean of material existence becomes like the water in a calf's hoofprint.'

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.51 Hi, because; [when actions are performed with an attitude of equanimity, it leads to becoming freed from sin etc. Therefore, by stages, it becomes the cause of Liberation as well.] buddhi-yuktah, those who are devoted to wisdom, who are imbued with the wisdom of equanimity; (they) becoming manisinah, men of Enlightenment; tyaktva, by giving up; phalam, the fruit, the acquisition of desirable and undesirable bodies; karmajam, produced by actions; gacchanti, reach; padam, the state, the supreme state of Visnu, called Liberation; anamayam, beyond evils.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads the verse as the destination-claim of buddhi-yoga. Those joined to wisdom (buddhi-yuktāḥ) become enlightened ones (manīṣiṇaḥ) by giving up the karma-born fruits — specifically, the acquisition of desirable and undesirable bodies (good rebirths and bad rebirths). They reach 'padam anāmayam' — the state beyond all evils — which Shankara names 'the supreme state of Viṣṇu, called Liberation'. The verse closes the buddhi-yoga sequence (2.49-2.51) with its terminus: stage-by-stage, equanimous action leads to release from saṁsāra. CRITICAL: 'janma-bandha' is metaphysical — bondage of birth-and-rebirth, not a generic 'bondage' of being stuck in habits. Engineering analog must NOT claim metaphysical liberation.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: not captured (raw page returned null body for Ramanuja on 2.51); summary inferred from neighboring verses (2.49, 2.50, 2.52) which are continuous in argument
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja's verbatim Sanskrit-bhasya for 2.51 was not extractable from bgus on the current pull (null body). However, the surrounding verses (2.49, 2.50, 2.52) and Ramanuja's general doctrinal frame on this block establish the reading: the wise (manīṣiṇaḥ) joined to buddhi-yoga abandon the fruit BORN OF ACTION (karma-jam phalam) — the accumulation that generates janma-bandha (bondage of saṁsāra) — and attain padam anāmayam, the state beyond affliction. Ramanuja reads the destination as devotional liberation (mokṣa as eternal service to the Supreme), not Advaitic merging. The structural claim — buddhi-yoga is the path; janma-bandha is what is dissolved; padam anāmayam is the goal — is shared with Shankara.

positions.rendering: the state beyond all miseries [by going back to Godhead]
the supreme state of Viṣṇu, called Liberation; beyond all evils
the state beyond affliction; devotional liberation as eternal service to the Supreme

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: padam anāmayam is the destination-clause. anāmayam is unambiguous: a-nā-maya, 'without disease/affliction'. padam can be read as 'state', 'position', 'abode', or 'place'. The disagreement is in how the destination is theologically named: Shankara names it 'the supreme state of Viṣṇu, called Liberation' (mokṣa, with theistic-Advaita frame); Ramanuja names it Vaikuṇṭha-style devotional liberation; Prabhupada explicitly glosses it as 'going back to Godhead' (Gaudiya Vaishnava); Mukundananda renders the lexical floor ('state beyond all suffering') without sectarian commitment. All four agree the destination is metaphysical — release from saṁsāra. Engineering layer must remain operational: stay close to operational truth (release from a specific kind of friction, not metaphysical liberation).

positions.rendering: the cycle of birth and death
the cycle of life and death
bondage of birth — desirable and undesirable embodiment, the saṁsāric chain
bondage of saṁsāra; the chain of births that result from karma-attached action

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All four traditions read janma-bandha as the saṁsāric chain — bondage TO birth-and-rebirth, generated by the karma-fruit accumulation that fruit-attached action creates. This is metaphysical, not metaphorical. Engineering layer must NOT translate 'janma-bandha' as 'being stuck in bad habits' or 'cycle of bad rollouts' — the verse is making a metaphysical claim about saṁsāra. The honest engineering analog: a specific kind of operational friction is dissolved by buddhi-yoga (not metaphysical liberation). Acknowledge the analog as STRETCHED in scope per the chapter's voice-and-restraint guidelines.

literal_meaning: Indeed, the wise joined to buddhi, having abandoned the fruit born of action, freed from the bondage of birth, reach the state without affliction.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 2.51 closes the buddhi-yoga sequence (2.49-2.51) by naming its destination. The structural claim has three steps. (1) Those joined to buddhi (buddhi-yuktāḥ) become enlightened ones (manīṣiṇaḥ). (2) They abandon the fruit born of action (karma-jaṁ phalam tyaktvā), which is what generates janma-bandha — bondage of birth-and-rebirth. (3) They reach padam anāmayam — the state without affliction. All traditions read this as metaphysical: Shankara as the supreme state of Viṣṇu (Liberation, mokṣa); Ramanuja as devotional liberation (Vaikuṇṭha-style, eternal service); Gaudiya Vaishnava as 'going back to Godhead'. The verse gives the WHY for the discipline introduced in 2.39 and ranked in 2.49: the discipline ends saṁsāra. Engineering layer must remain scope-honest. The metaphysical claim does NOT translate cleanly into 'better engineering outcomes' — what does translate is the operational analog: a specific kind of grasping-friction is released for the operator who is buddhi-yukta. Tag as STRETCHED if claiming more.

cross_references.explanation: 2.39 introduced karma-yoga as a separate discipline. 2.51 names its terminal: padam anāmayam, the state beyond affliction. The 2.39-2.51 arc is now closed at the destination level.
2.50 named the discipline (yogaḥ karmasu kauśalam — yoga is skillfulness in action). 2.51 names what that skill releases (janma-bandha) and what it reaches (padam anāmayam).

BG 5.10 — 'one who acts surrendering all actions to Brahman, abandoning attachment, is not touched by sin as a lotus leaf is not touched by water' — extends the 2.51 image of janma-bandha-vinirmuktāḥ. Engineering layers in chapter 5 use the lotus-leaf imagery; chapter 2 stays at the destination-naming level.

remaining_gaps: Ramanuja's verbatim Gita-bhasya on 2.51 not extractable from bgus on current pull (null body returned). Summary inferred from continuity of argument with surrounding verses 2.49, 2.50, 2.52.
Sridhara Swami and Vishvanatha Chakravarti commentaries available on bgus but not pulled into source pack.
